- **Heart failure**
- **Asystole** if the escape rhythm fails

---

## Step 6: Why look for a cause?

Because several causes are **reversible** — and the lecturer's own ECG indications list names them.

---

**Ischaemia** — especially **INFERIOR myocardial infarction**

>> **Why inferior?** Because in most people the **right coronary artery supplies both the inferior wall AND the AV node.** Block that artery and you get an inferior MI **plus** heart block, together.

>> **So in any patient with complete heart block, look hard at leads II, III and aVF.**

---

**Drugs** — **digoxin toxicity** and beta-blockers, both slow AV conduction. *("Drug effect e.g. digoxin toxicity" is on the indications list.)*

**Electrolytes** — **hyperkalaemia**, also on the list.

---

>> **Fix the cause and the block may resolve completely — no pacemaker needed.**

---

## Putting it all together

>> **P waves marching independently through the QRS = complete heart block.**

>> **Wide QRS = ventricular escape = unstable = urgent.**

>> **Atropine, then pacing.**

>> **And always look for inferior MI, digoxin and potassium.**
cards.tie:
This case draws on three separate ECG indications at once — **heart blocks**, **ischaemic heart disease** and **drug effect**.

And it shows why 'march out the P waves' is a skill worth practising.

cards.q: OSPE SIMULATION 5 — A 55-year-old man has crushing central chest pain for 2 hours. His ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III and aVF. What is the diagnosis, which artery is involved, and what investigations confirm it?
cards.lecturer:
**Diagnosis: Acute INFERIOR ST-elevation myocardial infarction.**

**Model answer:** "This ECG shows **ST segment elevation in leads II, III and aVF** — the **inferior** leads. In a patient with 2 hours of crushing central chest pain, this represents an **acute inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction**. I would look for **reciprocal ST depression** in leads I and aVL."

**Artery:** the **right coronary artery** in the majority of patients.

**Basis:** **ischaemic heart disease** is a listed indication for ECG.

**Confirmatory and supporting investigations:** **cardiac enzymes and troponins** — markers of myocardial injury, elevated in myocardial infarction; **troponin I and troponin T** are the specific markers, while CK, AST and LDH are the enzymes. **Echocardiography** — to look for regional wall motion abnormality and complications. **Coronary arteriography** — an invasive procedure using contrast agents for evaluation of the coronary arteries, useful in the **diagnosis and management** of coronary artery disease. Also send **lipid profile** and **blood sugar** as risk factors, and **electrolytes, urea and creatinine**.

**Watch for:** heart block, since the right coronary artery also supplies the AV node.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Localise from the leads — this is the whole skill

>> **Leads II, III and aVF = the INFERIOR surface of the heart.**

---

**Memorise the three territories. They come up constantly:**

| Leads | Territory | Usual artery |
|---|---|---|
| **II, III, aVF** | **INFERIOR** | **Right coronary** |
| **V1–V4** | **ANTERIOR** | Left anterior descending |
| **I, aVL, V5, V6** | **LATERAL** | Circumflex |

---

>> **Never just say "there is ST elevation." Say WHICH LEADS — because the leads name the territory, and the territory names the artery.**

---

## Step 2: Why does ST elevation mean infarction?

**Step 1:** A coronary artery is blocked

**Step 2:** The muscle it supplies loses its blood supply

**Step 3:** Injured muscle cannot maintain its normal resting charge

**Step 4:** A current flows between injured and healthy muscle

**Step 5:** This shifts the ST segment **upward** in the leads facing the injury

---

>> **ST elevation = full-thickness injury, happening NOW.** That is why it is an emergency — muscle is dying while you look at it.

---

## Step 3: Look for RECIPROCAL changes — this earns marks

>> **Reciprocal ST DEPRESSION in the leads OPPOSITE the infarct.**

---

**Why does it happen?**

**A lead on the opposite side of the heart is looking at the same injury current from behind.** The same electrical event that raises ST in one direction lowers it in the other.

---

| Infarct | Reciprocal changes in |
|---|---|
| **Inferior** (II, III, aVF) | **I and aVL** |
| **Anterior** (V1–V4) | Inferior leads |

---

>> **Why does this matter?** Because reciprocal change makes the diagnosis **much** more certain. It distinguishes a true infarct from **pericarditis**, where ST elevation is **widespread with NO reciprocal depression**.

---

## Step 4: The extra danger of an INFERIOR MI

This is the clinically important extra.

---

>> **In most people, the right coronary artery supplies BOTH the inferior wall AND the AV node.**

**Block it, and you can lose both at once:**

- Inferior wall infarction
- **AV block** — first degree, second degree, or **complete heart block**

---

>> **So in any inferior MI, watch the rhythm.** Bradycardia and heart block are common, and may need atropine or pacing.

**Two of the lecturer's ECG indications appearing in one patient.**

---

## Step 5: Which blood test confirms it, and why troponin?

>> Go back to the lecture: **"Cardiac enzymes and troponins are markers of myocardial injury. The markers are elevated in myocardial infarction."**

---

**Why prefer troponin over CK, AST and LDH?**

| Marker | Problem |
|---|---|
| **CK** | Also in **skeletal muscle** |
| **AST** | Also in the **liver** |
| **LDH** | In almost **every tissue** |
| **Troponin I and T** | **Essentially heart-specific** |

---

>> **The enzymes say "muscle damage somewhere." Troponin says "HEART muscle damage."**

---

**But note the crucial timing point:**

>> **Do NOT wait for the troponin to treat a STEMI.**

The ECG diagnosis is enough. Troponin takes hours to rise, and muscle is dying now.

**The ECG is the emergency test. The troponin is confirmation.**

---

## Step 6: What do the other investigations add?

---

**ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY**

>> Echo cannot see the coronary artery. But it shows the **wall that stopped moving** — a **regional wall motion abnormality** — and **where** it is confirms the territory.

It also finds **complications**: mitral regurgitation, ventricular septal rupture, mural thrombus, pericardial effusion.

---

**CORONARY ARTERIOGRAPHY**

>> **The only test that shows the artery itself.**

And it is *"useful in the diagnosis AND MANAGEMENT"* — because through the same catheter the narrowing can be **stented open**.

---

**LIPIDS and BLOOD SUGAR**

>> These do not diagnose the infarct. They identify the **risk factors** — objective 3 — that caused it and will cause the next one.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **II, III, aVF = inferior = right coronary artery.**

>> **Look for reciprocal depression in I and aVL — it confirms infarct rather than pericarditis.**

>> **Watch for heart block, because the same artery feeds the AV node.**

>> **Troponin confirms. Arteriography shows the artery. But treat on the ECG.**
cards.tie:
This station connects almost the whole lecture:

ECG (ischaemia) → troponin (injury marker) → echo (wall motion, complications) → arteriography (the artery) → lipids and sugar (risk factors).

**One patient, six investigations, each answering a different objective.**

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO — A 62-year-old hypertensive woman presents with progressive breathlessness on exertion and swelling of both legs for 3 weeks. She sleeps on three pillows. On examination she has raised JVP, bibasal crepitations and pitting pedal oedema. How do you investigate her, and what does each test contribute?
cards.lecturer:
**Working diagnosis: Congestive cardiac failure, likely hypertensive heart disease.**

**Investigations, and what each contributes:**

**Chest X-ray** — to diagnose **cardiomegaly** (CTR > 50%), to identify features of heart failure and hypertension, to diagnose **acute pulmonary oedema**, and to identify **precipitants of heart failure such as chest infection**.

**ECG** — for **chamber abnormalities such as ventricular hypertrophy and left atrial abnormality**, for **arrhythmias** (especially atrial fibrillation), and for evidence of **ischaemic heart disease** as a cause.

**Echocardiography** — the key test. To evaluate **cardiac structures, functions and dysfunctions**; indicated in **hypertensive heart disease** and **heart failure**; and to exclude **pericardial effusion**, valvular disease and **cardiac masses such as intramural thrombus**.

**BNP / NT-proBNP** — useful in the **screening and diagnosis of heart failure**, and **very useful in establishing prognosis**.

**Electrolytes, urea and creatinine** — to **exclude electrolytes as being responsible for the cardiovascular dysfunction**, and to **detect whether renal disease is present**.

**Lipid profile and blood sugar** — to identify **risk factors**.

**Cardiac MRI** may be considered — the **current gold standard for evaluation of left ventricular hypertrophy**.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Read the history — she has told you it is the heart

---

**"Breathlessness on exertion"** → reduced cardiac reserve

**"Sleeps on three pillows"** → **orthopnoea**

>> **Why does lying flat make it worse?** Fluid from the legs redistributes to the chest, and the diaphragm rises. Both worsen pulmonary congestion. Sitting up relieves it.

**"Swelling of both legs"** → **BILATERAL** — a systemic cause, not a local vein problem

---

**Examination confirms both sides of the heart:**

- **Raised JVP** and **pedal oedema** → **RIGHT** heart failure
- **Bibasal crepitations** → **LEFT** heart failure

>> **Both = CONGESTIVE cardiac failure.**

---

## Step 2: Now use the SEVEN OBJECTIVES to structure your investigations

>> This is what the framework is for. Do not list tests randomly — **assign each test to an objective.**

---

### Objective 1 — Establish the diagnosis

**BNP / NT-proBNP** → is this actually heart failure?

**Echocardiography** → confirms it, and shows the **ejection fraction**

**Chest X-ray** → cardiomegaly and pulmonary congestion

---

### Objective 2 — Determine the aetiology

>> **She is HYPERTENSIVE.** So the likely cause is **hypertensive heart disease** — which is the **first indication** on the echo list.

**Echo** shows left ventricular **hypertrophy**.

**ECG** shows **ventricular hypertrophy** and **left atrial abnormality** — both listed indications.

>> But do not stop there. **Also exclude ischaemic heart disease and valvular disease** — the echo covers both.

---

### Objective 3 — Identify risk factors

**Lipid profile** and **blood sugar**.

>> These will not diagnose her heart failure. They tell you **why her arteries are damaged** and what to modify.

---

### Objective 4 — Detect complications

**Echo** → look for **intramural thrombus** — a listed indication under cardiac masses. A dilated, poorly contracting ventricle allows blood to stagnate and clot.

**ECG** → look for **atrial fibrillation**, a common complication which also causes thrombus.

---

### Objective 5 — Evaluate co-morbidities

**Electrolytes, urea and creatinine** → **detect whether or not renal disease is present.**

>> Heart and kidney damage each other. And you are about to give her diuretics, so you need a baseline.

---

### Objectives 6 and 7 — Monitor and prognosticate

**BNP** → *"very useful in establishing prognosis of heart failure."*

And **repeat echo and BNP** track her over time.

---

## Step 3: Why is the ECHO the single most important test?

Because it answers a question nothing else can.

---

>> **Is this heart failure with REDUCED or PRESERVED ejection fraction?**

**Reduced** — a weak ventricle that cannot empty

**Preserved** — a stiff, hypertrophied ventricle that cannot **fill**

---

>> **In a long-standing hypertensive, a stiff hypertrophied ventricle is very likely.**

**These two are treated differently.** Only the echo distinguishes them.

**And only echo distinguishes cardiomegaly from a pericardial effusion** — remember the X-ray shows a shadow, not its contents.

---

## Step 4: The question everyone forgets to ask

>> **Why did she decompensate NOW?**

She has been hypertensive for years. **Something changed three weeks ago.**

---

**The lecture tells you to look:** *"to identify precipitants of heart failure e.g. **chest infection**."*

**Consider:** infection · **new atrial fibrillation** · silent ischaemia · anaemia · missed medication · high salt intake · thyroid disease

---

>> **The same chest X-ray that shows her heart failure may show you the pneumonia that caused it.**

**And treating the precipitant is often what actually gets her better.**

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Diagnose it** — BNP, echo, CXR

>> **Find the cause** — hypertension; but exclude ischaemia and valve disease

>> **Find the risk factors** — lipids, sugar

>> **Find the complications** — thrombus, AF

>> **Find the co-morbidities** — renal function

>> **And find out why today.**
cards.tie:
This is the seven objectives applied to a single real patient.

If you can assign every test you order to one of the seven, you will never be caught out by 'why did you request that?'

cards.q: OSPE SIMULATION 6 — You are shown an echocardiogram still image. There is a large black (echo-free) space surrounding the heart, separating it from the pericardium, and the right-sided chambers appear to collapse during part of the cycle. What is the diagnosis and what does it mean clinically?
cards.lecturer:
**Diagnosis: Large pericardial effusion with echocardiographic features of cardiac tamponade.**

**Model answer:** "This echocardiogram shows a large **echo-free (black) space surrounding the heart**, consistent with a **pericardial effusion**. There is **collapse of the right-sided chambers**, which suggests **cardiac tamponade** — the effusion is compressing the heart and impairing filling. This is a medical emergency."

**Basis:** echocardiography is indicated in **pericardial diseases, especially pericardial effusion**, and is used **to evaluate cardiac structures, functions and dysfunctions** and **to detect complications of cardiovascular diseases**.

**Why echo and not chest X-ray:** the chest X-ray can show an enlarged cardiac shadow, but **cannot distinguish an enlarged heart from fluid around a normal-sized heart**. Only echocardiography makes that distinction.

**Management:** urgent **pericardiocentesis** if there is tamponade; IV fluids while awaiting drainage; avoid diuretics and vasodilators. Investigate the cause — infection including **tuberculosis**, malignancy, uraemia (send **urea and creatinine**), connective tissue disease, and post-myocardial infarction. An **ECG** may show small complexes or electrical alternans.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Learn to read black and white on an echo

>> **On ultrasound: FLUID is BLACK. Tissue is grey. Bone and calcium are white.**

---

**Why is fluid black?**

Ultrasound passes straight through fluid without being reflected. **No reflection = no signal = black.**

---

>> **So a black space where there should be tissue means FLUID.**

A black rim **around the heart, inside the pericardium** = **pericardial effusion**.

---

## Step 2: Why can the chest X-ray not do this?

This is the point of the station.

---

**On chest X-ray, a big cardiac shadow can be:**

**1.** A **dilated** heart

**2.** A **hypertrophied** heart

**3.** A **normal heart surrounded by fluid**

---

>> **The X-ray shows an OUTLINE. It cannot see inside the outline.**

**The echo separates the muscle from the water.**

---

>> This is exactly why the lecturer writes **"pericardial diseases ESPECIALLY pericardial effusion"** in the echo indications — the word *especially* is doing real work.

---

**The X-ray clue, if you are given one:** a **globular, "water-bottle" shaped** heart with **clear lung fields**.

>> **Big heart WITH wet lungs** → heart failure. **Big heart with DRY lungs** → think effusion.

---

## Step 3: Why does chamber collapse mean TAMPONADE?

This is the finding that turns a diagnosis into an emergency.

---

**Step 1:** The pericardium is a **tough, fibrous sac** that does not stretch quickly

**Step 2:** Fluid accumulates inside it

**Step 3:** Pressure in the pericardial space **rises**

**Step 4:** When that pressure exceeds the pressure inside a chamber, the chamber is **squashed**

---

>> **Why the RIGHT side first?**

Because the right atrium and right ventricle are **low-pressure, thin-walled** chambers. **They collapse before the left.**

---

**Step 5:** Collapsed chambers **cannot fill**

**Step 6:** No filling → **no stroke volume**

**Step 7:** **Cardiac output falls** → shock → death

---

>> **Tamponade does not kill by squeezing the heart. It kills by preventing it FILLING.**

**That single sentence explains the entire management.**

---

## Step 4: Why the management is counter-intuitive

---

>> **GIVE IV FLUIDS.**

**A patient with a big heart and low blood pressure — and you give fluid?**

**Yes.** Because the problem is **inadequate filling**. Raising the filling pressure helps the chambers resist collapse. **It buys time.**

---

>> **AVOID diuretics and vasodilators.**

Both **reduce** filling pressure — the exact opposite of what this heart needs.

>> **This is the trap:** the patient looks like heart failure — raised JVP, hypotension, breathlessness. **Treat him as heart failure with a diuretic and you may kill him.**

---

>> **The definitive treatment is to REMOVE THE FLUID — pericardiocentesis.**

Nothing else works, because nothing else relieves the pressure.

---

## Step 5: The classic clinical signs to mention

**Beck's triad** of tamponade:

**1. Hypotension**

**2. Raised JVP**

**3. Muffled heart sounds** — fluid dampens the sound

---

**Plus pulsus paradoxus** — an exaggerated fall in systolic pressure on inspiration.

---

## Step 6: What caused it? — and one cause matters especially here

---

>> **TUBERCULOSIS.** In our setting, TB is a leading cause of pericardial effusion and constrictive pericarditis.

---

**Other causes:**

- **Malignancy**
- **Uraemia** — hence *"urea and creatinine... to detect whether or not renal disease is present"*
- Connective tissue disease
- Post-myocardial infarction
- Viral or bacterial pericarditis

---

**And the ECG may help:**

>> **Small complexes** (the fluid insulates the electrical signal) and **electrical alternans** (the heart swinging within the fluid, so complex height alternates beat to beat).

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Black rim around the heart = pericardial effusion.**

>> **Right-sided chamber collapse = TAMPONADE = emergency.**

>> **It kills by preventing FILLING — so give fluid, never diuretics.**

>> **Drain it. And in our setting, think TB.**
cards.tie:
This station is the clearest demonstration of why echo exists.

The chest X-ray raised the question — big shadow.

**Only the echo answered it: muscle, or water?**

cards.q: KEY CUES — What phrases and findings should trigger a specific answer in cardiology investigation questions?
cards.lecturer: Summary of the discriminating features and figures drawn across the whole lecture.
cards.explain:
## The classification cue

**"Limited to the body surface"** → **NON-INVASIVE** — 12-lead ECG, **transthoracic** echo

**"Penetration of vascular system or body tissues"** → **INVASIVE** — EP studies, catheterization, **transoesophageal** echo, angiography

>> **The trap: echo is in BOTH. Transthoracic = non-invasive. Transoesophageal = invasive.**

---

## THE NUMBERS — learn these cold

| Figure | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **CTR > 50%** | Cardiomegaly (adults) |
| **Transverse cardiac diameter > 15.5 cm** | Cardiomegaly (adults) |
| **12 leads · 10–20 seconds** | Standard ECG |
| **24 hours** | **Holter / ambulatory** ECG |
| **BNP = 32 amino acids** | From the **ventricles** |
| **NT-proBNP = 76 amino acids** | Co-secreted with BNP |
| **25 mm/s · 10 mm/mV** | ECG calibration |
| **1 small square = 0.04 s** | ECG paper |
| **PR normal 0.12–0.20 s** | 3–5 small squares |
| **QRS normal < 0.12 s** | Under 3 small squares |

---

## Chest X-ray cues

**"Is there cardiomegaly?"** → check **PA not AP** first · then **CTR > 50%**

**"AP film"** → **CANNOT assess heart size** — the heart is magnified

**"Upper lobe venous diversion"** → **earliest sign of left heart failure**

**"Blunted costophrenic angles"** → **pleural effusions**

**"Kerley B lines"** → interstitial pulmonary oedema

**"Globular / water-bottle heart with clear lungs"** → **pericardial effusion** → get an **ECHO**

>> **Heart failure on CXR: ABCDE — Alveolar oedema · B lines · Cardiomegaly · Diversion · Effusions**

---

## ECG pattern cues — instant recognition

**"Irregularly irregular, no P waves"** → **ATRIAL FIBRILLATION**

**"Sawtooth baseline"** → atrial flutter

**"Tall tented T waves"** → **HYPERKALAEMIA** → treat before the lab result

**"Flat T waves + U waves"** → **HYPOKALAEMIA**

**"Short PR + delta wave"** → **WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE**

**"P waves independent of QRS"** → complete heart block

**"Wide QRS ≥ 0.12 s"** → **bundle branch block**

**"PR > 0.20 s"** → first degree heart block

**"Downsloping ST, reverse tick"** → **DIGOXIN effect**

**"Widespread saddle ST elevation"** → pericarditis

**"Tall QRS complexes"** → **ventricular hypertrophy**

---

## ST territory cues

| Leads | Territory |
|---|---|
| **II, III, aVF** | **INFERIOR** |
| **V1–V4** | **ANTERIOR** |
| **I, aVL, V5, V6** | **LATERAL** |

---

## Axis shortcut

| Lead I | aVF | Axis |
|---|---|---|
| Up | Up | **Normal** |
| Up | Down | **Left** deviation |
| Down | Up | **Right** deviation |

>> **Right axis deviation** → think **cor pulmonale**, right ventricular hypertrophy

---

## "Which test?" cues

**"Racing heart, intermittent"** → **HOLTER** (24 hours)

**"Chest pain on exertion only"** → **STRESS ECG**

**"Is the ventricle thick?"** → echo shows it; **CARDIAC MRI is the GOLD STANDARD for LVH**

**"Measure the pressure inside the heart"** → **CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION** (only test that can)

**"See the coronary arteries"** → **CORONARY ARTERIOGRAPHY** (only test that can)

**"Close an ASD/VSD without surgery"** → **CATHETERIZATION** — the only investigation that also **treats**

**"Vegetations / small structures / atrial thrombus"** → **TRANSOESOPHAGEAL** echo

**"Is this breathlessness cardiac?"** → **BNP** — a normal level makes heart failure unlikely

**"Big cardiac shadow — muscle or fluid?"** → **ECHO**

---

## Blood test cues

**"Marker of myocardial injury"** → cardiac enzymes and troponins

**"CK, AST, LDH"** → enzymes — **sensitive but NOT specific to heart**

**"Troponin I and T"** → **specific to heart muscle**

**"Produced by the ventricles, 32 amino acids"** → **BNP**

**"Prognosis in heart failure"** → **BNP / NT-proBNP**

---

## Supportive test cues

**"Fasting lipid profile"** → **TC · LDL · HDL · TG** — fasting because **triglycerides rise after meals**

**"FBS and 2HPPS"** → blood sugar, to exclude **diabetes mellitus**

**"Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, HCO₃⁻"** → electrolytes — two reasons: **exclude electrolytes as the cause of the cardiac dysfunction**, and **detect renal disease**

---

## The two "only" facts

>> **Only CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION measures intracardiac pressure — and only it can also treat.**

>> **Only CORONARY ARTERIOGRAPHY shows the coronary arteries themselves.**

---

## The gold standard fact

>> **CARDIAC MRI is the current gold standard for evaluation of LEFT VENTRICULAR HYPERTROPHY** — and it uses **no ionizing radiation**.
